drafts.email1:
Hi Dave,

I came across NCT07399132 — your EARLY/Phase 1 study of A Study of APL-10456-Vaccine, and wanted to reach out.

Early-phase vaccine programmes are where the dose-immunogenicity relationship needs to be established — characterising antibody titres and seroconversion rates across dose levels is what drives the pivotal trial design. With 144 patients across the dose-escalation and expansion cohorts, there's a real opportunity to build a robust PopPK model early that directly informs RP2D selection — rather than retrofitting it later under time pressure.

Our team at PumasAI — pharmacometricians and clinical pharmacologists, several from FDA, Pfizer, and BMS — has delivered 40+ regulatory submissions across FDA, EMA, PMDA, and Health Canada, all accepted on first review. We handle the full clinical pharmacology programme end-to-end: NCA, PopPK, dose-escalation modelling, PK/PD characterisation, and regulatory writing through submission.

Would a short call make sense to see if there's a fit? Happy to share a relevant case study from a similar EARLY/Phase 1 Vaccines programme beforehand.
drafts.subject2: Re: NCT07399132 — Early-Phase PK & Dose Characterisation
drafts.fu1:
Hi Dave,

Wanted to follow up briefly on my earlier note about NCT07399132.

One thing I often hear from Phase 1 teams is that PK data interpretation bottlenecks the dose decision timeline. Our modelling platform compresses turnaround from weeks to days — worth 15 minutes to walk through how?

We recently supported a Phase 1 FIH programme where our NCA + PopPK package directly informed the dose-escalation committee, getting to RP2D two months ahead of the original timeline — happy to share a short write-up if it'd be useful context for your team. No pitch, just the technical detail.

I came across NCT06997627 — your Phase 1, Phase 2 study of A Phase I/II Study to Evaluate the Safety and Immunogenicity of GC3111B in Healthy Adults, and wanted to reach out.

Early-phase rare disease programmes often have very limited patient numbers to work with. Model-informed dose escalation helps extract maximum pharmacological insight from every subject, which is critical when recruitment is inherently constrained. With 120 patients across the dose-escalation and expansion cohorts, there's a real opportunity to build a robust PopPK model early that directly informs RP2D selection — rather than retrofitting it later under time pressure.

Our team at PumasAI — pharmacometricians and clinical pharmacologists, several from FDA, Pfizer, and BMS — has delivered 40+ regulatory submissions across FDA, EMA, PMDA, and Health Canada, all accepted on first review. We handle the full clinical pharmacology programme end-to-end: NCA, PopPK, dose-escalation modelling, PK/PD characterisation, and regulatory writing through submission.

Would a short call make sense to see if there's a fit? Happy to share a relevant case study from a similar Phase 1, Phase 2 Vaccines programme beforehand.
drafts.subject2: Re: NCT06997627 — Early-Phase PK & Dose Characterisation
drafts.fu1:
Hi Hyeon,

Wanted to follow up briefly on my earlier note about NCT06997627.

One thing I often hear from Phase 1 teams is that PK data interpretation bottlenecks the dose decision timeline. Our modelling platform compresses turnaround from weeks to days — worth 15 minutes to walk through how?

We recently supported a Phase 1 FIH programme where our NCA + PopPK package directly informed the dose-escalation committee, getting to RP2D two months ahead of the original timeline — happy to share a short write-up if it'd be useful context for your team. No pitch, just the technical detail.

Would it be worth 15 minutes to see if the timing works for GC Biopharma Corp?

drafts.email1:
Hi Bochao,

I came across NCT07055880 — your Phase 2 study of Immunogenicity and Safety of Different Dosages of Rabies Vaccine (Serum-free Vero Cell), and wanted to reach out.

Phase 2 is where the exposure-response relationship becomes the central question — the E-R for both efficacy and toxicity needs to be well-characterised before committing to a Phase 3 dose. Increasingly, FDA expects a rigorous E-R package before you even start the pivotal trial. With 120 patients, you'll have a dataset that supports a strong PopPK/PD model, and getting that analysis right now is often the difference between a clean Phase 3 dose rationale and a regulatory back-and-forth that delays the pivotal trial.

Our team at PumasAI — pharmacometricians and clinical pharmacologists, several from FDA, Pfizer, and BMS — has delivered 40+ regulatory submissions across FDA, EMA, PMDA, and Health Canada, all accepted on first review. We handle the full clinical pharmacology programme end-to-end: PopPK, exposure-response, dose optimisation, clinical trial simulation, and regulatory writing through submission.

Would a short call make sense to see if there's a fit? Happy to share a relevant case study from a similar Phase 2 Vaccines programme beforehand.

Hi Lucas,

I came across NCT07055880 — your Phase 2 study of Immunogenicity and Safety of Different Dosages of Rabies Vaccine (Serum-free Vero Cell), and wanted to reach out.

Phase 2 is where the exposure-response relationship becomes the central question — the E-R for both efficacy and toxicity needs to be well-characterised before committing to a Phase 3 dose. Increasingly, FDA expects a rigorous E-R package before you even start the pivotal trial. With 120 patients, you'll have a dataset that supports a strong PopPK/PD model, and getting that analysis right now is often the difference between a clean Phase 3 dose rationale and a regulatory back-and-forth that delays the pivotal trial.

Our team at PumasAI — pharmacometricians and clinical pharmacologists, several from FDA, Pfizer, and BMS — has delivered 40+ regulatory submissions across FDA, EMA, PMDA, and Health Canada, all accepted on first review. We handle the full clinical pharmacology programme end-to-end: PopPK, exposure-response, dose optimisation, clinical trial simulation, and regulatory writing through submission.

Would a short call make sense to see if there's a fit? Happy to share a relevant case study from a similar Phase 2 Vaccines programme beforehand.
drafts.subject2: Re: NCT07055880 — Exposure-Response & Phase 3 Dose Rationale
drafts.fu1:
Hi Lucas,

Wanted to follow up briefly on my earlier note about NCT07055880.

The difference between a clean Phase 3 package and a prolonged FDA review often comes down to the quality of the E-R analysis done in Phase 2. Happy to share how we've navigated that for similar programmes.

We recently completed an exposure-response analysis for a Phase 2 asset that directly informed the pivotal trial dose selection — the sponsor said it helped them avoid a Phase 3 protocol amendment — happy to share a short write-up if it'd be useful context for your team. No pitch, just the technical detail.

Would it be worth 15 minutes to see if the timing works for Sinovac Biotech Co., Ltd?

Phase 3 vaccine programmes are on the submission runway, and the clinical pharmacology package for BLA — immunogenicity modelling, exposure-response for seroconversion, lot consistency — is typically on the critical path to CBER review. With 4000 patients generating a rich dataset, this is the point where a well-executed clinical pharmacology package can keep the PDUFA date on track, or a gap in the analysis can trigger an information request that costs months.

Our team at PumasAI — pharmacometricians and clinical pharmacologists, several from FDA, Pfizer, and BMS — has delivered 40+ regulatory submissions across FDA, EMA, PMDA, and Health Canada, all accepted on first review. drafts.email1:
Hi James,

I came across NCT07284654 — your Phase 3 study of Safety, Tolerability, and Immunogenicity of VAX-31 in Adults ≥50 Years With Immunobridging to Adults 18-49, and wanted to reach out.

Phase 3 vaccine programmes are on the submission runway, and the clinical pharmacology package for BLA — immunogenicity modelling, exposure-response for seroconversion, lot consistency — is typically on the critical path to CBER review. With 4000 patients generating a rich dataset, this is the point where a well-executed clinical pharmacology package can keep the PDUFA date on track, or a gap in the analysis can trigger an information request that costs months.

Our team at PumasAI — pharmacometricians and clinical pharmacologists, several from FDA, Pfizer, and BMS — has delivered 40+ regulatory submissions across FDA, EMA, PMDA, and Health Canada, all accepted on first review. drafts.subject1: Summit Therapeutics — MIDD & Clinical Pharmacology Support
drafts.email1:
Hi H,

I came across your Oncology programme, and wanted to reach out.

Model-Informed Drug Development is increasingly expected by FDA and EMA reviewers — and programmes that build MIDD into their clinical strategy from the start consistently reach decision points faster with fewer surprises.

At PumasAI, we partner with clinical development teams to deliver model-informed drug development, clinical pharmacology, and regulatory strategy. Our scientists are pharmacometricians and clinical pharmacologists — former Pfizer, FDA, and academia — and we work end-to-end from model development through regulatory submission.

Across your 4-country footprint, regulatory-ready PK/PD packages are a recurring need — something we handle routinely.

Would a 20-minute call be worthwhile to see if there's a fit? Happy to share a brief case study relevant to your programme first.
drafts.subject2: Re: Summit Therapeutics — MIDD & Clinical Pharmacology Support
drafts.fu1:
Hi H,

Wanted to follow up briefly on my previous note.

Many teams we work with find that proactive MIDD strategy reduces overall development time and strengthens regulatory interactions. Happy to share a relevant example for your programme.

We recently supported a clinical development programme with PK/PD modelling that supported a key regulatory decision, reducing back-and-forth with FDA by several months — I'd be glad to share a short summary if it would be useful context for your team.

No commitment — just a conversation to see if the timing makes sense for Summit Therapeutics.

Still interested in a quick call?
